stem: A 44-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP with an 8-month history of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Cycles are regular at 28 days but bleeding now lasts 9 days with flooding through super tampons hourly on days 2-3 and passage of large clots up to 3 cm in size. She reports increasing tiredness, palpitations on minor exertion, and exertional shortness of breath climbing one flight of stairs. Her work as a primary school teacher has been disrupted and she has had to change pads during teaching sessions. She is not currently sexually active, has had no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, and no postcoital spotting. There is no abdominal or pelvic pain. There is no easy bruising, no gum bleeding last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. BMI is 34. She has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and takes no anticoagulants, no antiplatelets and no over-the-counter NSAIDs. She has had two normal vaginal deliveries with no postpartum haemorrhage. She has not yet had pelvic imaging or endometrial sampling in this presentation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. Bimanual: uterus bulky, mobile, anteverted, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L (115-165), MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 mcg/L. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: In addition to a transvaginal ultrasound and full blood count with ferritin, which further investigation is most important at this visit?

options.text: FSH and oestradiol to assess for perimenopausal anovulation as cause
options.rationale: Random FSH/oestradiol in a 44-year-old with regular cycles is unhelpful; cycle regularity argues against anovulation. Hormonal testing is correct only with established cycle irregularity (RANZCOG HMB).

options.text: Coagulation screen and von Willebrand panel as the first-line bleeding work-up
options.rationale: Inherited bleeding disorder screen is for HMB since menarche or personal/family bleeding cues; her 8-month history without bleeding red flags does not prioritise this. Coag/vWF panel is correct with bleeding history clues (RANZCOG HMB).

options.text: Endometrial Pipelle biopsy at this visit, with TSH and coagulation screen as adjuncts
options.rationale: RANZCOG and NICE NG88 recommend endometrial sampling in women with HMB who are aged 45 or over OR who have risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer; obesity (BMI 34) is a major risk factor and TVUS alone is insufficient to exclude endometrial pathology in this setting (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy as the sole investigation in place of TVUS today
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is appropriate where TVUS suggests focal pathology or sampling is unsuccessful, but TVUS remains first-line. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only with TVUS showing focal lesion (NICE NG88).

options.text: Serum CA-125 to screen for occult ovarian malignancy in this patient
options.rationale: CA-125 is non-specific in premenopausal women and has no role in HMB workup. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Workup of HMB follows PALM-COEIN. TVUS plus FBC/ferritin are universal. Endometrial sampling is indicated at age >=45, persistent intermenstrual bleeding, failed medical therapy, or risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, anovulation, tamoxifen, Lynch). TSH and coagulation screen are adjuncts based on history.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
FBC + ferritin in all; TSH if cycle change or clinical features; coags if bleeding history since menarche
Biopsy if age >=45 OR risk factor for endometrial cancer (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen)
TVUS first-line imaging; saline-infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy for focal lesions
explanation.pearls: Obesity lowers the age threshold for endometrial sampling. Don't anchor on age 45 if other risk factors are present.

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - structured PALM-COEIN workup

stem: A 39-year-old G1P1 woman has a 2-year history of heavy regular menstrual bleeding with cycle-related bulk symptoms including urinary frequency, mild lower-abdominal pressure, and occasional nocturia. She passes large clots during menses and floods through super tampons every 1-2 hours on the heaviest days. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm intramural fibroid (FIGO type 4, not distorting the cavity) and a 3 cm subserosal fibroid; the endometrial cavity is regular and the endometrium is 6 mm. Endometrial biopsy returns benign proliferative endometrium with no atypia. She has completed her family, is in a monogamous relationship using condoms for contraception, and would prefer to avoid surgery if possible. She does not want to lose her fertility surgically and is open to a long-acting hormonal device. She has tried tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses with only modest improvement and has not tolerated mefenamic acid. BMI 26.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, lower-uterine mass palpable just above pubis. Bimanual: bulky irregular uterus approx 12-week size, mobile.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 101 g/L (115-165), ferritin 14 mcg/L. TVUS as above. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?

options.text: GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly) with add-back oestrogen
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are used short-term (3-6 months) to shrink fibroids pre-surgery or to bridge perimenopause; not first-line ongoing therapy due to vasomotor symptoms and bone loss. GnRH is correct as bridge therapy, not chronic (RANZCOG).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion now
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is first-line medical therapy for HMB including fibroid-related HMB where the uterine cavity is not distorted by submucosal fibroids; it reduces blood loss by 70-95% and is acceptable for a woman who has completed her family (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: TXA reduces blood loss by 40-50% but does not address bulk symptoms and is less effective than LNG-IUS overall. TXA is correct for acute heavy periods or as adjunct (NICE NG88).

options.text: Mefenamic acid 500 mg orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: NSAIDs reduce blood loss by ~25-30% and help dysmenorrhoea; useful adjuncts but not first-line for moderate-severe HMB with anaemia. NSAIDs are correct as adjunctive symptomatic therapy (NICE NG88).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy under GA
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but should be reserved for failed/declined medical therapy; she has not yet trialled LARC. Hysterectomy is correct after failed conservative options or with patient preference (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: For fibroid-related HMB without significant cavity distortion: LNG-IUS first-line. Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs are useful adjuncts. GnRH analogues are short-term pre-surgical or perimenopausal bridging agents. Myomectomy preserves fertility; hysterectomy is definitive when family complete and other options failed.
key_points: LNG-IUS: first-line for HMB without cavity distortion (FIGO types 3-7 generally OK)
Submucosal fibroids (FIGO 0-2) distorting cavity: hysteroscopic myomectomy
GnRH agonist: short-term shrinkage pre-op or perimenopausal bridge
Uterine artery embolisation: alternative when fertility not desired
Myomectomy: preserves fertility; hysterectomy: definitive cure
explanation.pearls: Mirena requires a cavity that the device can sit in. A large submucosal fibroid distorting the cavity contraindicates Mirena and instead triggers hysteroscopic myomectomy.

subtopic_detail: Uterine fibroids - choice of medical therapy

stem: A 27-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 4 years of cyclical pelvic pain (peaks day 1-2, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia during menses, cyclical back pain). She has tried regular ibuprofen and heat-pack regimen with minimal benefit. She is not planning conception in the next 18-24 months but does want children eventually. She has no migraine with aura, no smoking, no VTE history, BMI 24. Pelvic USS normal (no endometrioma). Bimanual examination shows tender uterosacral nodularity. She is now keen for the most evidence-based next step that does not commit her to surgery if it can be avoided.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile, mildly tender. No palpable nodularity in the pouch of Douglas. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal-appearing ovaries, no endometrioma, no deep infiltrating lesion visualised.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial continuous combined oral contraceptive pill with NSAID for breakthrough pain, with review at 3-6 months and laparoscopy if refractory or fertility planning advances
options.rationale: RANZCOG and ESHRE endometriosis guidance support empirical hormonal therapy (continuous COCP or progestogen) as first-line in suspected endometriosis without requiring laparoscopic confirmation, especially if fertility is not imminently sought. Laparoscopy is reserved for refractory cases or fertility planning (RANZCOG; ESHRE 2022).

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before any medical therapy
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy is now preferred first-line over diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis. Laparoscopy is correct after failed medical therapy or for surgical planning (ESHRE 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Serum CA-125 to support the working diagnosis
options.rationale: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology, not endometriosis diagnosis (ESHRE 2022).

options.text: MRI pelvis as the next investigation to confirm the working diagnosis
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis with surgical planning, not primary diagnosis. MRI is correct for deep endometriosis surgical mapping (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: GnRH agonist with add-back HRT as first-line therapy
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second/third line after COCP/progestogens due to bone density and menopausal symptoms. GnRH is correct after first-line hormonal failure (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Endometriosis affects about 1 in 9 Australian women and is consistently diagnosed late, with average symptom-to-diagnosis interval of 7-12 years. Cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, cyclical bowel or bladder symptoms, and subfertility are the core presentation. Examination may show tender nodularity of the uterosacral ligaments or a fixed retroverted uterus, but is often normal. Transvaginal ultrasound detects endometriomas and deep infiltrating disease in expert hands but is normal in superficial peritoneal disease - so a normal scan does not exclude endometriosis. Laparoscopy with histological confirmation is the gold standard but is not required to start empirical therapy. First-line medical management is NSAIDs plus continuous combined oral contraceptive (or POP or LNG-IUS), with 3-6 month review of symptom response. Second-line is GnRH agonist with add-back HRT, or dienogest. Laparoscopic excision is for confirmed disease, treatment-failure, infertility workup, or endometrioma over 3 cm.
key_points: Classic features: cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria
Empirical hormonal therapy precedes laparoscopy in straightforward presentations
TVUS first-line imaging; MRI for suspected deep disease
Laparoscopy: failed medical therapy, suspected deep disease, infertility, severe symptoms
Refer to gynaecologist if no response in 3-6 months or red flags
explanation.pearls: Endometriosis is a clinical diagnosis - TVUS is normal in superficial peritoneal disease, MRI and CA-125 add nothing to first-line management. Trial continuous COCP plus NSAID and review at 3-6 months; laparoscopy is for treatment failure or fertility concerns (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

stem: A 33-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea following a 12-month history of progressively irregular cycles. She reports new hot flushes (around 6-8 per day), troublesome night sweats with disturbed sleep, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia, low mood, reduced libido and increased fatigue. She is in a stable long-term relationship and is open to future fertility consideration, although they have not actively been trying to conceive. There is no recent significant weight change, no excessive exercise, no medication changesoms. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no previous ovarian surgery. She had menarche at age 12 with previously regular 28-day cycles. Family history: her mother had menopause at age 41 and her maternal aunt had menopause in her early forties; one cousin has Hashimoto's thyroiditis. BMI 23. She is not using contraception currently. Beta-hCG is negative today. Initial bloods 5 weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L, oestradiol low, prolactin normal, TSH normal, total testosterone normal. Today's repeat FSH and oestradiol have been collected and are pending.

data_table.Examination: BMI 23. No goitre. No visual field defect. Speculum: pale dry vaginal mucosa.
data_table.Investigations: Five weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L (premenopausal <10), oestradiol low, LH 38, prolactin normal, TSH 1.8, beta-hCG negative. Today: bloods repeated, pending.

lead_in: If today's repeat FSH is similarly elevated (>25 IU/L), which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure and recheck FSH in 6 months as a single elevated FSH is non-diagnostic alone
options.rationale: POI diagnosis requires two FSH >25 IU/L at least 4 weeks apart; her first sample was 5 weeks prior, so confirmatory second now is appropriate. Reassurance with re-check at 6 months is correct only with isolated borderline FSH and no amenorrhoea (Australasian Menopause Society POI).

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill solely for menopausal symptom control without further workup needed
options.rationale: COCP can be used in POI but HRT provides more physiological replacement and better bone protection. COCP is correct when contraception desired alongside replacement (BMS).

options.text: Bone density scan and fertility counselling referral, but defer hormonal therapy until age 40 years
options.rationale: Delaying oestrogen replacement until 40 risks cardiovascular, bone, and neurocognitive harm; replacement should commence at diagnosis. Defer HRT is essentially never correct in confirmed POI (ESHRE).

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for an adrenal failure work-up before initiating any hormonal therapy in her
options.rationale: Adrenal autoimmune screen (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is part of POI workup but does not need to precede HRT commencement. Adrenal screen is correct concurrently, not before HRT (ESHRE).

options.text: Systemic HRT, fertility counselling, screen for adrenal/thyroid antibodies and karyotype, plus baseline bone densitometry
options.rationale: POI confirmed by FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions at least 4 weeks apart with amenorrhoea >=4 months in a woman <40 mandates HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause to protect bone and cardiovascular health; concurrent work-up includes karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal antibodies, thyroid antibodies, and a fertility discussion (ESHRE 2016; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: POI: amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea >=4 months + FSH >25 IU/L on 2 readings 4+ weeks apart, in a woman aged under 40. Work-up: karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal (21-OH) and thyroid antibodies, baseline DXA. Treat with HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause (~51) for bone, CV and neurocognitive protection. Spontaneous pregnancy possible in ~5-10%; HRT is not a contraceptive.
key_points: Diagnosis: amenorrhoea >=4 months + 2 x FSH >25 IU/L, 4+ weeks apart, age <40
Causes: idiopathic, genetic (Turner, fragile X), autoimmune, iatrogenic (chemo/radiotherapy/surgery)
HRT (or COCP) until ~age 51
Discuss contraception separately - HRT not contraceptive
Refer for fertility counselling early
explanation.pearls: POI is not just early menopause - women may still ovulate intermittently and conceive; HRT does not prevent pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Premature ovarian insufficiency - diagnosis and HRT

stem: A 21-year-old sexually active woman presents with 4 days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, mucopurulent vaginal discharge, fever 38.4 C, HR 110, BP 100/64. Examination: bilateral lower-quadrant tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, AND a 6 cm tender right adnexal mass with bedside USS demonstrating a multilocular cystic mass with internal debris consistent with tubo-ovarian abscess. She is unable to tolerate oral intake due to nausea and vomiting. Urine beta-hCG negative. WCC 18, CRP 180. NAAT swabs sent. She has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen: bilateral lower abdominal tenderness. Bimanual: cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, no palpable adnexal mass.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit and commence IV ceftriaxone, doxycycline (or azithromycin if intolerant) and metronidazole, with surgical or interventional drainage if no improvement at 48-72 hours
options.rationale: Severe PID with tubo-ovarian abscess, systemic features (fever, tachycardia, vomiting) requires hospital admission and IV combination therapy. Drainage is added if there is no response in 48-72 hours or the abscess is large (RANZCOG PID; eTG).

options.text: Outpatient ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus oral doxycycline plus oral metronidazole for 14 days despite the imaging-confirmed tubo-ovarian abscess here
options.rationale: Outpatient regimen is for mild-moderate PID without abscess or systemic features; her TOA, fever, tachycardia, vomiting require IV in hospital. Outpatient is correct in mild PID (RANZCOG PID; eTG).

options.text: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone and discharge home with safety-net advice despite the imaging-confirmed tubo-ovarian abscess on the recent pelvic scan
options.rationale: Single-dose monotherapy is inadequate for any PID, let alone severe with TOA. Single-dose IM is essentially never sufficient in PID (eTG).

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparoscopy and salpingectomy as the primary intervention before any trial of intravenous antibiotic therapy for the abscess
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, ruptured TOA, or failure of antibiotic therapy at 48-72 hours. Surgery is correct after antibiotic failure or rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin monotherapy for 14 days despite the polymicrobial pelvic infection with anaerobic and gonococcal coverage gaps in this regimen
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is not first-line PID therapy and does not cover gonorrhoea, anaerobes, or atypicals. Amoxicillin is correct in confirmed susceptible Listeria or strep infections, not PID (eTG).

explanation.summary: Empirical outpatient PID regimen per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat + doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Take partner notification, STI screen, and HIV/hepatitis screening at presentation.
key_points: Ceftriaxone covers gonorrhoea
Doxycycline covers chlamydia and M. genitalium (test/retest if treatment failure)
Metronidazole covers anaerobes
Admit and treat IV if pregnant, severe, TOA, sepsis, or failed oral therapy
Partner notification and contact tracing essential
explanation.pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis - don't wait for swab results before starting antibiotics.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia25. She does not smoke. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Well. BP 118/74. BMI 25. Soft well-involuted uterus, no lochia, perineum healed. Lactating breasts, no mastitis.
data_table.Investigations: None required today; routine 6-week postnatal review.

lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as BP has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 <6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding, UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months. CHC is correct >6 months postpartum or not breastfeeding (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole contraceptive method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
options.rationale: LAM requires all three: amenorrhoea, exclusive/near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND <6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter. LAM is correct when all three criteria met (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted today, and etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
options.rationale: Intrauterine contraception (LNG-IUS or Cu-IUD) can be safely inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (UKMEC 1-2); progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA) are also suitable in breastfeeding women from any time postpartum (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an adverse effect on milk supply
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant does not adversely affect milk supply and can be inserted at any time postpartum. Implant delay is essentially never required (FSRH; WHO MEC).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding and should not be used at all here
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 1 from 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 before). DMPA is correct from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception in breastfeeding women: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS) are all safe at 6 weeks. Cu-IUD or LNG-IUS can be inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (or immediately postpartum within 48 h of birth). CHC is avoided <6 weeks (UKMEC 4) and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months while breastfeeding. LAM is ~98% effective only when ALL three criteria are met: <6 months, amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding.
key_points: POP can start day 21 postpartum without need to wait
IUD/IUS: insert immediately postpartum (within 48 h) or from 4-6 weeks
CHC: avoid <6 weeks if breastfeeding; from 6 weeks UKMEC 2; from 6 months UKMEC 1
DMPA: UKMEC 2 before 6 weeks if breastfeeding, then UKMEC 1
LAM strict criteria: amenorrhoea + exclusive BF + <6 months postpartum
explanation.pearls: Day 21 postpartum is the earliest fertility could return in non-breastfeeding women - that's why POP and CHC have day 21 as the earliest start if other criteria allow.

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception in a breastfeeding woman

stem: A 38-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her general practice. She is not pregnant, has had two previous CSTs at ages 28 and 33 which were both negative for oncogenic HPV, and has had no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleedingpelvic pain. She is in a stable monogamous relationship of 11 years and uses the LNG-IUS 52 mg (Mirena, inserted 4 years ago for both contraception and HMB control) and is currently amenorrhoeic. She has had a total of 4 lifetime sexual partners and has never had a sexually transmitted infection identified. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14 through the school-based program. Her practitioner-collected sample returns: oncogenic HPV detected (non-16/18 type); reflex liquid-based cytology performed automatically by the laboratory shows no intraepithelial lesion or malignancy. She is immunocompetent and has no history of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cervical excisional treatment. She does not smoke.

data_table.Examination: Routine speculum normal at time of collection.
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: oncogenic non-16/18 detected. Reflex LBC: negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy.

lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Direct referral for colposcopy with cervical biopsy now
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy is for HPV 16/18 regardless of cytology, or any oncogenic HPV with pHSIL/HSIL, or persistent HPV at 24 months. Direct colposcopy is correct in those higher-risk pathways (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 12 months (intermediate-risk pathway)
options.rationale: Oncogenic non-16/18 HPV with reflex LBC negative or low-grade abnormality places the patient in the intermediate-risk group; repeat HPV at 12 months. If still HPV-positive then refer to colposcopy; if negative, return to 5-yearly screening (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly cervical screening immediately
options.rationale: 5-yearly screening resumes only after HPV becomes negative; any oncogenic HPV detection triggers 12-month repeat or colposcopy. Routine 5-yearly is correct only with negative HPV (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV test for partial genotype in 6 months
options.rationale: 6-month repeat is not NCSP-recommended; intermediate-risk repeat is at 12 months. 6-month repeat was the old pathway, now obsolete (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Refer for excisional treatment (LLETZ) without further investigation
options.rationale: Treatment is based on histological diagnosis from colposcopy/biopsy, not on HPV status with negative cytology. LLETZ is correct after histology confirms high-grade dysplasia (NCSP 2022).

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP: HPV 16/18 detected -> direct colposcopy regardless of LBC. Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade reflex LBC -> repeat HPV in 12 months (intermediate risk). Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with possible high-grade or worse on LBC -> colposcopy. Persistent oncogenic HPV at 24 months -> colposcopy.
key_points: HPV 16/18 (any LBC): direct colposcopy
Other oncogenic HPV + negative/low-grade LBC: repeat HPV at 12 months
Other oncogenic HPV + possible high-grade or worse LBC: colposcopy
If 12-month repeat HPV negative -> return to 5-yearly screening
Self-collected vaginal swab is now universally available since July 2022
explanation.pearls: Reflex LBC is performed automatically by the lab if oncogenic HPV is detected; the practitioner does not need to order it separately.

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening - oncogenic non-16/18 HPV management

stem: A 62-year-old G3P3 woman presents to her general practice with a 6-week history of intermittent painless vaginal bleeding. She has been menopausal since age 51 (last menstrual period at 51) and has had no episodes of bleeding in the intervening 11 years until now. The current bleeding is light to moderate, requires a pad most days, and has been ongoing intermittently for the last 6 weeks. There is no associated abdominal or pelvic pain, no urinary symptoms, no bowel change and no weight loss. She has never been on menopausal hormone therapy, has never used tamoxifen, and has no family history of endometrial, ovarian, breast or bowel cancer (specifically no features of Lynch syndrome on family history). Her BMI is 33, she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1 g twice daily (HbA1c 6.6%), and her hypertension is well controlled on perindopril 5 mg daily. She has had three previous spontaneous vaginal births, none complicated, and no postpartum haemorrhage. Her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. She is on no anticoagulants and no antiplatelets. A transvaginal ultrasound is being arranged today.

data_table.Examination: BMI 33. Speculum: small amount of dark blood at cervix, no visible cervical lesion. Bimanual: uterus mobile, normal size, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240. UEC and LFTs normal. Beta-hCG not indicated. Cervical screening negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Regarding TVUS endometrial thickness in this clinical setting, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: If TVUS endometrial thickness is <=2 mm then endometrial sampling is mandatory regardless of other factors
options.rationale: Endometrial thickness <=4 mm has very low cancer risk and TVUS alone may be sufficient initial triage; sampling not mandatory below this cut-off. Mandatory sampling at <=2 mm is over-treatment (RANZCOG).

options.text: A TVUS endometrial thickness of 3 mm essentially excludes endometrial cancer and no further investigation is needed even if recurrent bleeding
options.rationale: Although thin endometrium has high NPV, recurrent or persistent PMB requires sampling regardless of TVUS thickness. Reassurance without sampling is correct only for single PMB episode with thin endometrium and resolution (RCOG).

options.text: Tamoxifen use makes TVUS thickness measurement unreliable due to subepithelial cysts; saline sonohysterography or hysteroscopy is preferred
options.rationale: True but not directly relevant - she is not on tamoxifen. This option is a red herring (RANZCOG).

options.text: She should proceed directly to hysteroscopy without prior TVUS, given her constellation of risk factors
options.rationale: TVUS is appropriate first-line imaging; hysteroscopy is for thickened endometrium >=4 mm, recurrent PMB, or non-diagnostic Pipelle. Direct hysteroscopy is correct after TVUS shows thickened or focal endometrium (RCOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Endometrial thickness >=4 mm on TVUS warrants endometrial sampling (Pipelle), with hysteroscopy if focal lesions or non-diagnostic sampling
options.rationale: The widely used 4 mm cut-off has a sensitivity of ~95% for endometrial carcinoma in postmenopausal bleeding; endometrial sampling is indicated above this threshold, with hysteroscopy +/- directed biopsy if focal pathology is suspected or Pipelle is non-diagnostic (RCOG Green-top 67; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: TVUS endometrial thickness <=4 mm has a high negative predictive value (~99%) for endometrial cancer in PMB; sampling not mandatory if bleeding is a single episode. Thickness >=4 mm triggers Pipelle biopsy +/- hysteroscopy. Recurrent PMB warrants sampling regardless of TVUS. Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, nulliparity, late menopause, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome.
key_points: TVUS endometrial thickness >=4 mm -> Pipelle biopsy
Recurrent PMB -> sample regardless of TVUS thickness
Hysteroscopy if focal lesion, non-diagnostic Pipelle, or thickened endometrium
Risk factors: obesity, DM, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen
Approximately 10% of PMB has endometrial cancer
explanation.pearls: Any PMB warrants investigation. A thin endometrium does not equal a benign outcome if bleeding recurs.

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness threshold

stem: A 53-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her GP with 18 months of disabling vasomotor symptoms: 8-12 hot flushes per day with drenching night sweats requiring change of nightwear most nights, fragmented sleep with average 4-5 hours, persistently low mood (PHQ-9 score 11), and reduced libido. She has tried fan, cooling clothing, regular exercise, alcohol avoidance and trigger-diary approaches with limited benefit. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago and she is therefore postmenopausal by clinical criteria. She has an intact uterus and has had no episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. Her BMI is 31, BP 132/84 mmHg, fasting lipids in target range and HbA1c 5.5%. She had a single provoked DVT after a long-haul flight 8 years ago and was anticoagulated for 3 months; no thrombophilia was identified and she has had no subsequent VTE. She has no personal or family history of breast cancer, no ischaemic heart disease, and no migraine with aura. She does not smoke. Cervical screening and mammography are up to date and normal. She does not want a monthly bleed and would prefer a no-bleed regimen if possible.

data_table.Examination: BMI 31. BP 132/84. Breast examination normal. Pelvic examination unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting lipids: total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.1. HbA1c 5.5%. TSH 1.6 mIU/L. Mammogram normal 10 months ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate MHT regimen for her?

options.text: Oral oestradiol 1 mg daily plus cyclical medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 12 days each month
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogen produces monthly bleed and is preferred in perimenopausal women still cycling; this woman is >12 months postmenopausal preferring no-bleed. Cyclical regimen is correct in perimenopausal HRT (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily plus continuous medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg orally
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk ~2-fold; transdermal at standard doses does not. Oral CEE is correct only in women without VTE risk factors (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg orally once daily as monotherapy for menopausal symptom control alone
options.rationale: Tibolone is reasonable for vasomotor and libido symptoms but increases stroke risk (especially age >60) and is not first-line in women with elevated stroke risk. Tibolone is correct in younger women without stroke risk factors (BMS).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h twice weekly plus levonorgestrel 52 mg IUD (Mirena)
options.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and does not increase VTE risk at standard doses, making it the preferred route in women with prior VTE, obesity or other VTE risk factors. The LNG-IUS provides effective endometrial protection in a no-bleed manner. Together this provides combined MHT for a woman with an intact uterus (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h applied twice weekly alone, with no progestogen at all
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia/cancer; progestogen mandatory. Oestrogen-alone is correct only after hysterectomy (BMS; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: MHT principles: oestrogen alone if hysterectomy; combined (oestrogen + progestogen) if uterus intact. Cyclical progestogen for perimenopausal women still having periods; continuous combined for women >=12 months postmenopausal who want no bleed. Transdermal oestrogen preferred when VTE risk is elevated (prior VTE, obesity, age) - no first-pass hepatic effect. LNG-IUS is an effective progestogen for endometrial protection.
key_points: Uterus intact -> add progestogen (mandatory)
Hysterectomy -> oestrogen alone
Cyclical progestogen if still cycling; continuous if >12 months amenorrhoea
Transdermal oestrogen: no increased VTE risk; preferred if VTE risk factors
Absolute contraindications: current/recent breast cancer, oestrogen-dependent malignancy, undiagnosed PV bleeding, active VTE/PE, active liver disease, untreated endometrial hyperplasia
explanation.pearls: If a woman has a uterus and you forget the progestogen, you cause endometrial cancer. LNG-IUS plus transdermal oestrogen is an elegant solution in many women.

stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to her GP with 18 months of progressive vaginal dryness, deep dyspareunia and urinary urgency without incontinence. She has had three episodes of culture-confirmed E. coli urinary tract infection in the past 12 months, each requiring antibiotics, with no upper-tract involvement. She had a left-sided early-stage T1N0 oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer treated with breast-conserving lumpectomy, sentinel node biopsy, adjuvant radiotherapy and 5 years of adjuvant tamoxifen completed 12 months ago. She did not receive an aromatase inhibitor. She is currently in remission with no evidence of disease on her most recent annual surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago. She is not on systemic MHT. She has tried over-the-counter water-based vaginal lubricants for intercourse and a non-hormonal hyaluronic acid-based vaginal moisturiser (Replens) used three times weekly consistently for 4 months with only modest symptomatic benefit. She is sexually active with her long-term partner and her quality of life and intimate relationship are significantly affected.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: pale thin atrophic vaginal mucosa, friable, loss of rugae. No discharge, no lesions. Bimanual: normal-sized uterus, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS today: no growth. Last surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago: no evidence of disease.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Avoid all oestrogen-containing therapies indefinitely and continue non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers and lubricants only
options.rationale: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen has minimal systemic absorption; recent observational data suggest no increased recurrence in non-AI users. Strict avoidance is correct only on aromatase inhibitor therapy or with patient/oncology preference (BMS; Jean Hailes).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Commence systemic oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h plus an LNG-IUS for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Systemic MHT is generally avoided in breast cancer survivors; local low-dose vaginal oestrogen is more appropriate and effective for GSM. Systemic MHT is correct only in selected refractory vasomotor symptoms with oncology consent (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Trial low-dose vaginal oestriol cream after discussion with breast oncologist, continuing non-hormonal moisturisers
options.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen in a breast cancer survivor not on aromatase inhibitors is appropriate after shared decision-making with the oncology team; severe GSM significantly affects quality of life and the systemic absorption is minimal. Current evidence does not show increased recurrence risk for most survivors. She is on no current endocrine therapy (completed tamoxifen 12 months ago) (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes; NAMS 2020).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Refer for fractional CO2 vaginal laser treatment as first-line alternative in this patient
options.rationale: Vaginal laser therapy lacks high-quality efficacy/safety evidence; TGA and international societies caution against use outside trials. Laser is correct only in clinical trial settings (TGA; NAMS).
source_refs: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

options.text: Commence ospemifene orally as the first-line therapy for her dyspareunia in this setting
options.rationale: Ospemifene is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer due to oestrogen agonist effects on breast tissue. Ospemifene is correct in postmenopausal dyspareunia without breast cancer history (FDA labelling).

explanation.summary: GSM (vaginal atrophy + urinary symptoms) responds well to low-dose vaginal oestrogen with minimal systemic absorption. In breast cancer survivors, local vaginal oestrogen is increasingly used after shared decision-making with the oncologist, especially in those not on aromatase inhibitors and with significant symptom burden. Non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants are first-line; vaginal oestrogen is added when symptoms persist.
key_points: First-line: non-hormonal moisturisers (Replens) + lubricants
Second-line: low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or oestradiol pessary
Local vaginal oestrogen: minimal systemic absorption
In breast cancer survivors: shared decision with oncology; tamoxifen users generally acceptable; AI users more contentious
GSM also reduces recurrent UTIs in postmenopausal women
explanation.pearls: Recurrent UTIs in a postmenopausal woman are often a manifestation of GSM and respond to vaginal oestrogen better than to recurrent antibiotic courses.

sources.label: Jean Hailes: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause

subtopic_detail: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause - local oestrogen

stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman has a 6-year established diagnosis of vulval lichen sclerosus, biopsy-confirmed at the time of diagnosis. She has been managed with topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment using a structured regimen (nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance long-term) and has been reasonably symptom-controlled until recently, with annual review by her gynaecologist. Over the past 4 months she has noticed a persistent fissured area on the right labium majus that has not healed despite optimisation of her steroid technique (correct application, adequate quantity confirmed at recent review). Symptoms include localised pruritus that has worsened, occasional spotting when wiping after micturition, and increasing discomfort with intercourse despite use of personal lubricant. She has no postcoital bleeding aside from the localised spotting described. She is on no immunosuppressive medications and is otherwise well, with controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. There is no history of previous vulval intraepithelial neoplasia, no previous vulval surgery, and her last cervical screening 18 months ago was negative for oncogenic HPV.

data_table.Examination: Vulva: classic white atrophic 'cigarette paper' skin extending in a figure-of-eight around vulva and perianal area, loss of labia minora architecture. New finding: 1.2 cm hyperkeratotic, fissured, indurated plaque on right labium majus that has not healed.
data_table.Investigations: None done today; review appointment for chronic LS surveillance.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase topical clobetasol propionate to nightly application for a further 8 weeks and review symptoms
options.rationale: Unhealed, indurated, or hyperkeratotic lesion in longstanding lichen sclerosus must NOT be assumed steroid-responsive; biopsy required to exclude differentiated VIN/SCC. Steroid increase is correct only after biopsy excludes neoplasia (BSSVD).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Add topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as a second-line steroid-sparing agent for symptomatic control of disease
options.rationale: Topical tacrolimus is recognised second-line steroid-sparing agent, but priority here is biopsy of the suspicious lesion before changing therapy. Tacrolimus is correct after biopsy excludes neoplasia (BSSVD).

options.text: Reassure that surveillance is not required after 5 years of stable disease and discharge to self-management with PRN steroid only
options.rationale: Lichen sclerosus carries 4-5% lifetime vulval SCC risk; long-term surveillance with prompt biopsy of suspicious lesions is recommended. Reassurance is essentially never correct in lichen sclerosus (BAD).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the suspicious vulval lesion without prior histology being obtained
options.rationale: Diagnostic biopsy precedes any wide excision; excisional treatment depends on histology. Wide excision is correct after histological confirmation of malignancy (BSSVD; RCOG).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology/vulval clinic for punch biopsy of the persistent fissured lesion, continuing topical clobetasol and at least annual surveillance
options.rationale: Persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, or non-healing lesion in established lichen sclerosus requires prompt biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN (the precursor most associated with LS-related vulval SCC, typically HPV-negative) and invasive SCC. Long-term annual surveillance is recommended due to ~4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC. Topical potent steroid (clobetasol) remains the cornerstone of medical management (BSSVD 2018; RCOG; Cancer Council).
source_refs: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis with a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma (via differentiated VIN, typically HPV-negative). Management: potent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05% ointment) induction then maintenance, plus long-term clinician surveillance. Any persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration or non-healing lesion warrants prompt biopsy. Self-management alone is inappropriate due to malignancy risk.
key_points: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment is the gold-standard topical therapy
Structured induction then maintenance (typically twice weekly long-term)
Lifetime vulval SCC risk ~4-5% via differentiated VIN (HPV-negative pathway)
Biopsy: persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, non-healing lesion
At least annual clinician review long-term, more frequent if uncontrolled or atypical features
Tacrolimus second-line; surgery reserved for adhesions/scarring or malignancy
explanation.pearls: Differentiated VIN looks innocuous and is easy to miss - any unhealed area in LS deserves a biopsy regardless of how unremarkable it appears.

sources.label: RCOG: The management of vulval skin disorders

subtopic_detail: Lichen sclerosus - surveillance and malignancy risk
